1
Chapter 01  ·  Statistical Inference

Population, Sample,
and the Sampling Distribution

Building the statistical foundations of data science — from first principles to real-world inference

Learning Objectives

  • Understand what populations and parameters are, and why we can almost never observe them directly
  • Distinguish between an estimator (a random variable) and an estimate (a number)
  • Master the sampling distribution — the distribution of a statistic across all possible samples
  • Derive and interpret the standard error formula $\text{SE}(\bar{X}) = \sigma/\sqrt{n}$
  • Prove why the sample variance requires dividing by $n - 1$, not $n$
  • Apply the Central Limit Theorem to construct confidence intervals
  • Distinguish four sampling methods and understand their trade-offs in data science projects
  • Recognize and diagnose sampling bias — and understand why more data cannot fix it

Contents

  1. 1.1   Population, Sample, and the Problem of Inference
    1. Populations, parameters, and the notation convention
    2. What is a sample? The i.i.d. assumption
    3. Estimators vs. estimates — a crucial distinction
    4. Why we sample — a data science perspective
  2. 1.2   The Sampling Distribution — The Central Concept
    1. What is a sampling distribution?
    2. The expectation of the sample mean — unbiasedness
    3. The variance of the sample mean — the standard error
    4. Estimating population variance — why $n - 1$?
    5. Degrees of freedom and the intuition
    6. The Normal approximation — the Central Limit Theorem
    7. How large a sample is needed?
    8. 1.5   Sampling Distributions from a Normal Population
      1. Distribution of the sample mean — exact result
      2. The chi-squared distribution of $S^2$
      3. Joint distribution of $\bar{X}$ and $S^2$ — independence
      4. Student's $t$ distribution
    9. 1.6   Sampling from a Finite Population
      1. The finite population correction factor
      2. When does the FPC matter?
    10. Sampling methods: simple random, stratified, cluster, systematic
    11. Sampling bias — and why it destroys inference
  3. Chapter Summary & Exercises

1.1 Population, Sample, and the Problem of Inference

Every quantitative question in data science ultimately targets something we cannot fully observe. A product manager asks: "What fraction of all our users would click this button?" A doctor asks: "Does this drug reduce blood pressure across all patients with this condition?" A data engineer asks: "What is the true average latency of our API endpoint?" In each case, the answer lives in a vast collection of values — past, present, and future — that we will never measure in full.

This is the fundamental problem of statistical inference: drawing reliable conclusions about an unknown whole from an observed part. The theory we develop in this chapter is the machinery for doing this carefully and honestly.

1.1.1 Populations, Parameters, and the Notation Convention

We begin with precise definitions, because sloppy language here causes confusion throughout.

Definition · Population

The population is the complete collection of all units (people, transactions, measurements, observations) that are relevant to our question. We model the population as a probability distribution $F_\theta$ — a mathematical description of how likely each possible value is. The subscript $\theta$ indicates that the shape of this distribution depends on one or more unknown quantities called parameters.

The population does not need to be finite or even enumerable. For example, the population of all future credit card transactions is infinite — it has not happened yet. Nevertheless, we model it as a distribution, and that distribution has unknown parameters we want to estimate.

Definition · Parameter

A parameter $\theta$ is a fixed, unknown numerical quantity that characterizes the population distribution. It lives in a parameter space $\Theta$. Common examples:

  • $\mu \in \mathbb{R}$ — the population mean (e.g., true average transaction value)
  • $\sigma^2 \in \mathbb{R}_{>0}$ — the population variance (e.g., spread in user session lengths)
  • $p \in [0,1]$ — a population proportion (e.g., true click-through rate)
  • $\lambda \in \mathbb{R}_{>0}$ — a rate parameter (e.g., true arrival rate of server requests)
  • $\boldsymbol{\beta} \in \mathbb{R}^k$ — a vector of regression coefficients

The defining feature of a parameter is that it is fixed — not random. The true click-through rate $p$ of a button is some specific number, say 0.127. We do not know it, but it exists. Our data are random; $\theta$ is not.

Intuition · The Greek/Roman Convention

Statisticians follow a typographic convention that tells you at a glance whether something is known or unknown:

  • Greek letters ($\mu, \sigma, \lambda, \theta, \beta$) → population parameters → fixed but unknown
  • Roman letters or "hat" notation ($\bar{X}, s, \hat{p}, \hat{\lambda}, \hat{\beta}$) → computed from data → random and observed

So $\mu$ is the true mean we want to know; $\bar{X}$ is the mean we compute from our sample. $\sigma$ is the true standard deviation; $s$ is our estimate. Learning to read this convention instantly tells you which quantities are known and which need to be estimated.

1.1.2 What Is a Sample? The i.i.d. Assumption

A sample is a collection of $n$ observations drawn from the population. We write the sample as $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n$. But not all samples are created equal — the mechanism by which we collect data has profound consequences for what conclusions we can legitimately draw.

Definition · Independent and Identically Distributed (i.i.d.) Sample

We say $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n$ is an i.i.d. sample from distribution $F_\theta$ if two conditions hold:

  1. Identically distributed: Each observation $X_i$ follows the same distribution $F_\theta$. This means every unit in the population had an equal chance of being selected — there is no systematic tendency to pick certain types of units over others.
  2. Independent: The observations give no information about each other. Knowing $X_1 = 42$ tells us nothing about what $X_2$ will be. Formally, the joint density factors completely: $f(x_1, \ldots, x_n \mid \theta) = \prod_{i=1}^n f(x_i \mid \theta)$.

These two assumptions, taken together, are the i.i.d. assumption. It is the foundation on which almost all classical statistical theory rests. When it is violated, our conclusions can be deeply wrong.

Why does independence matter so profoundly? Suppose you want to estimate average customer satisfaction for an e-commerce platform. If you survey 500 customers but they all come from one angry Reddit thread about a shipping problem, those 500 observations are highly correlated — they all share a common cause that makes them atypically negative. You have not gathered 500 independent pieces of information; you have gathered one piece of information (the Reddit thread) with 500 echoes. The effective sample size is much smaller than 500, and your estimate of average satisfaction will be wildly wrong.

Data Science Case · Independence Violations in Time-Series Data

In machine learning, a common mistake is to randomly split a time-series dataset into train and test sets, then report test accuracy. If your data is daily stock prices and you train on days 1–700 and test on a random subset including days from the same period, the train and test sets are highly correlated — prices on nearby days share market conditions, trends, and autocorrelation structure.

The result is that test accuracy looks excellent but the model fails completely in forward deployment. The correct approach is a temporal split: train on all data up to some cutoff date, test on data after that date. This preserves the independence structure relevant to the actual prediction task.

Similarly, if your dataset contains multiple rows per user (e.g., multiple purchases), rows from the same user are correlated. Randomly splitting such data leaks information — the model sees some purchases from user A in training and can "memorize" user A's patterns, then predict user A's test purchases almost perfectly. This is target leakage caused by i.i.d. violation.

1.1.3 Estimators vs. Estimates — A Crucial Distinction

This distinction is frequently glossed over but is essential for clear thinking.

Definition · Estimator vs. Estimate

An estimator $\hat{\theta}_n$ is a function of the sample — a rule for computing a guess for $\theta$ from any possible dataset of size $n$:

$$\hat{\theta}_n = T(X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n)$$

Because the sample $X_1, \ldots, X_n$ is random (different samples from the same population give different values), the estimator is also a random variable. It has its own probability distribution, its own expected value, and its own variance.

An estimate is the specific number we get after plugging in our observed data $x_1, \ldots, x_n$:

$$\hat{\theta} = T(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)$$

The estimate is a fixed number — it is not random. The estimator is the abstract rule; the estimate is what that rule produces on our specific dataset.

Example 1.1 · Estimator vs. Estimate for the Sample Mean

The sample mean estimator is the rule: "average all the observations." As a formula with uppercase letters (random variables):

$$\bar{X}_n = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n X_i \quad \text{(estimator — a random variable)}$$

If we collect heights of $n = 5$ adults and observe $\{172, 168, 180, 165, 175\}$ cm, the estimate is:

$$\bar{x}_5 = \frac{172 + 168 + 180 + 165 + 175}{5} = \frac{860}{5} = 172 \text{ cm} \quad \text{(estimate — a fixed number)}$$

The estimator $\bar{X}_5$ is a random variable with its own distribution (the sampling distribution we will study in Section 1.2). The estimate $172$ cm is one realization of that random variable — the specific value it took for this particular sample.

Why does this matter? Because to evaluate whether $\bar{X}$ is a good estimator of $\mu$, we need to think about its behavior across all possible samples — not just the one we happened to collect. Questions like "Is this estimator biased?" or "How precise is it?" are questions about the random variable $\bar{X}_n$, answered by studying its distribution. That distribution is called the sampling distribution, and it is the subject of the entire next section.

1.1.4 Why We Sample — A Data Science Perspective

If we could observe the entire population, we would not need any of this theory — we could just compute $\theta$ directly. So why do we sample? There are five fundamental reasons, each relevant to different data science contexts.

ReasonDescriptionData Science Example
Population is infinite The population includes future observations that do not yet exist Estimating future click-through rates; predicting tomorrow's traffic
Cost and time Measuring every unit is prohibitively expensive or slow Drug trials (n = 500 patients, not all 330M Americans); user surveys
Destructive testing Measurement destroys the unit, so you cannot test everything Hardware failure testing (burning out chips to find failure rates)
Computational limits Even with data on all units, computation on all is infeasible Training ML models on data too large for memory; mini-batch SGD
Practical sufficiency A well-designed sample of $n = 1{,}500$ can represent 330M people National polls; Netflix recommendation A/B tests with sample of users
💡
The power of sampling in modern data science: In 2021, Meta (Facebook) began running A/B experiments on a sample of roughly 1% of users rather than rolling out features to everyone at once. A 1% sample of ~3 billion users is still 30 million people — far more than any classical survey. Yet by treating it as a sample from a larger population (all future users, all possible conditions), they apply exactly the theory in this chapter to decide whether a new feature truly improves the product or whether the observed improvement could be due to chance.
Figure 1.1. The population of 5,000 adult heights (left) has a fixed, unknown true mean $\mu$ (gold dashed line). We draw a random sample of $n = 80$ (right). Our sample mean $\bar{x}$ (green dashed) is close to but not identical to $\mu$ (shown also in blue dotted for comparison). This gap is the sampling error. It is irreducible — any sample will have some — but it can be characterized and minimized through the theory we develop in Section 1.2.

1.2 The Sampling Distribution — The Central Concept

We said that an estimator is a random variable. What does that mean in practice? It means that if you repeated your data collection process many times — drawing a fresh random sample each time and computing $\hat{\theta}$ each time — you would get a different number every time. The sampling distribution is the probability distribution of all those possible values. Understanding it is the key to all of statistical inference.

The sampling distribution answers questions like: "On average, does my estimator hit the right target?" (unbiasedness), "How much does it jump around?" (standard error), and "What shape does it have, so I can make probability statements?" (Central Limit Theorem). Every concept in this section flows from this one idea.

1.2.1 What Is a Sampling Distribution?

Definition · Sampling Distribution

The sampling distribution of an estimator $\hat{\theta}_n$ is the probability distribution of $\hat{\theta}_n$ across all possible random samples of size $n$ from the population $F_\theta$.

It is the distribution you would get if you could repeat the following experiment infinitely many times:

  1. Draw a fresh random sample $X_1, \ldots, X_n$ from the population
  2. Compute the estimate $\hat{\theta}_n = T(x_1, \ldots, x_n)$
  3. Record the result

The histogram of all those recorded values, in the limit, is the sampling distribution.

Example 1.2 · Building a Sampling Distribution by Simulation

Suppose our population is exponentially distributed with mean $\mu = 5$ — a reasonable model for server response times in milliseconds. We cannot observe this whole population. But we can simulate it to understand the sampling distribution of $\bar{X}$.

The code below generates 10,000 samples of size $n = 30$ and plots the histogram of sample means:

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# True population: Exponential with mean = 5 (rate = 1/5)
true_mean = 5.0
population = np.random.exponential(scale=5, size=500_000)

n = 30          # sample size
B = 10_000      # number of repeated samples

# Draw B samples of size n, compute the mean of each
sample_means = [np.mean(np.random.choice(population, size=n))
                for _ in range(B)]

print(f"Mean of sample means: {np.mean(sample_means):.4f}  (true μ = {true_mean})")
print(f"Std of sample means:  {np.std(sample_means):.4f}  (theory: σ/√n = {5/np.sqrt(n):.4f})")

# Mean of sample means: 5.0012  (true μ = 5.0)   ← unbiased!
# Std of sample means:  0.9118  (theory: σ/√n = 0.9129) ← matches theory!

Notice: the mean of the sample means equals the true population mean (unbiasedness), and the spread of the sampling distribution matches the theoretical formula $\sigma/\sqrt{n}$ exactly.

Figure 1.2. Sampling distributions of $\bar{X}$ for four sample sizes, when the population is exponentially distributed (right-skewed, mean = 5). With $n = 2$, the sampling distribution inherits the skewness of the population. By $n = 30$, it is nearly symmetric and bell-shaped. By $n = 100$, it is tightly concentrated and indistinguishable from normal (black dashed curve). All four distributions are centered at $\mu = 5$ — this is the unbiasedness property we prove next. The standard error (SE, shown in each title) shrinks systematically as $n$ grows.

Three patterns in Figure 1.2 are worth internalizing, because they form the backbone of everything that follows:

  1. All sampling distributions center at the true $\mu$. The estimator $\bar{X}$ is "right on average" regardless of $n$. We formalize this as unbiasedness in Section 1.2.2.
  2. The sampling distribution narrows as $n$ increases. Individual estimates cluster more tightly around the truth with more data. We quantify this narrowing with the standard error in Section 1.2.3.
  3. The shape approaches normal regardless of the population. Even though the underlying data is skewed, $\bar{X}$ looks normal for large $n$. This is the Central Limit Theorem, which we prove in Section 1.2.5.

1.2.2 The Expectation of the Sample Mean — Unbiasedness

The first and most natural question about any estimator: is it aimed at the right target? That is, if we averaged our estimator over all possible samples, would we get the true parameter? This property is called unbiasedness.

Definition · Bias and Unbiasedness

The bias of an estimator $\hat{\theta}_n$ is the systematic deviation of its expected value from the true parameter:

$$\text{Bias}(\hat{\theta}_n) = \mathbb{E}[\hat{\theta}_n] - \theta$$

The expectation here is taken over all possible samples of size $n$ from $F_\theta$. An estimator is unbiased if $\text{Bias}(\hat{\theta}_n) = 0$ for all $\theta \in \Theta$, i.e., $\mathbb{E}[\hat{\theta}_n] = \theta$.

Unbiasedness does not mean individual estimates are correct. It means that the distribution of estimates is centered at the truth. Individual estimates can be anywhere in the distribution — some above $\theta$, some below — but they balance out to $\theta$ on average.

Theorem 1.1 · $\mathbb{E}[\bar{X}_n] = \mu$  —  The Sample Mean is Unbiased

For any population with finite mean $\mu$, the sample mean $\bar{X}_n = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n X_i$ satisfies $\mathbb{E}[\bar{X}_n] = \mu$.

Proof

We apply two properties of expectation that hold completely generally:

  • Linearity: $\mathbb{E}[aY + bZ] = a\mathbb{E}[Y] + b\mathbb{E}[Z]$ for constants $a, b$ and any random variables $Y, Z$
  • Identical distributions: Since all $X_i \sim F_\theta$, each has the same mean: $\mathbb{E}[X_i] = \mu$ for all $i$
$$\mathbb{E}[\bar{X}_n] = \mathbb{E}\!\left[\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n X_i\right] \stackrel{\text{linearity}}{=} \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n \mathbb{E}[X_i] \stackrel{\text{i.d.}}{=} \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n \mu = \frac{n\mu}{n} = \mu \qquad \blacksquare$$

Note that this proof uses only the "identically distributed" part of i.i.d., not the independence part. So unbiasedness holds even for correlated samples. Independence is needed for the variance calculation below. ■

Intuition · What Unbiasedness Means for a Data Scientist

Suppose you run a survey every month to estimate customer satisfaction. If $\bar{X}$ is unbiased for $\mu$, then across all the months — across all the different random samples you will ever draw — your estimates will be centered on the true satisfaction level. Some months you will overestimate (you happened to sample unusually happy customers), some months you will underestimate, but on average you are right.

This is valuable but not sufficient. An estimate that is unbiased but extremely variable is still unreliable for any particular month. That is why we also care about variance — the topic of the next subsection.

1.2.3 The Variance of the Sample Mean — The Standard Error

Knowing that $\bar{X}$ is centered at $\mu$ tells us the estimator points in the right direction. But how spread out is the sampling distribution? How much does $\bar{X}$ vary from one sample to the next? This is captured by the variance of the sampling distribution, or equivalently, the standard error.

Definition · Standard Error

The standard error (SE) of an estimator $\hat{\theta}_n$ is the standard deviation of its sampling distribution:

$$\text{SE}(\hat{\theta}_n) = \sqrt{\text{Var}(\hat{\theta}_n)}$$

Standard error lives on the same scale as $\hat{\theta}_n$ (e.g., cm if estimating height, dollars if estimating income), making it the natural measure of the estimator's precision.

Critical distinction: The standard deviation $\sigma$ of the population describes how individual observations vary. The standard error describes how the sample mean varies across different samples. They measure fundamentally different things.

Theorem 1.2 · $\text{Var}(\bar{X}_n) = \sigma^2/n$  —  The Variance of the Sample Mean

If $X_1, \ldots, X_n$ are independent with the same variance $\sigma^2$, then:

$$\text{Var}(\bar{X}_n) = \frac{\sigma^2}{n} \qquad \text{and} \qquad \text{SE}(\bar{X}_n) = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$
Proof

We need two variance rules. For a constant $c$ and random variable $Y$: $\text{Var}(cY) = c^2\text{Var}(Y)$. For independent random variables $Y_1, \ldots, Y_n$: $\text{Var}(Y_1 + \cdots + Y_n) = \text{Var}(Y_1) + \cdots + \text{Var}(Y_n)$. Independence is essential for this second rule — it fails for correlated variables.

$$\text{Var}(\bar{X}_n) = \text{Var}\!\left(\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n X_i\right) = \frac{1}{n^2}\text{Var}\!\left(\sum_{i=1}^n X_i\right) \stackrel{\text{independence}}{=} \frac{1}{n^2}\sum_{i=1}^n \text{Var}(X_i) = \frac{1}{n^2}\cdot n\sigma^2 = \frac{\sigma^2}{n}$$

Taking the square root: $\text{SE}(\bar{X}_n) = \sigma/\sqrt{n}$. ■

Intuition · The $\sqrt{n}$ Law — The Most Important Formula in Applied Statistics

The formula $\text{SE} = \sigma/\sqrt{n}$ encodes the law of diminishing returns in sampling. Here is why the square root appears — not just as a algebraic accident, but from the structure of averaging:

  • When you add $n$ independent random variables, their variances add: $\text{Var}(\sum X_i) = n\sigma^2$.
  • But we then divide by $n$ to get the mean, which divides variance by $n^2$: $\text{Var}(\bar{X}) = n\sigma^2/n^2 = \sigma^2/n$.
  • Standard deviation is the square root: $\text{SE}(\bar{X}) = \sigma/\sqrt{n}$.

The squaring from the variance definition and the $n^2$ from the $1/n$ factor conspire to give us $\sqrt{n}$ in the denominator.

Practical implications for experiment design:

  • To halve the SE, you need 4× as many observations ($\sqrt{4} = 2$)
  • To reduce SE by 90%, you need 100× as many observations
  • Going from $n = 100$ to $n = 200$ cuts SE by 29% — large gain
  • Going from $n = 10{,}000$ to $n = 10{,}100$ cuts SE by 0.5% — negligible gain

This explains why well-designed studies focus on collecting enough data to reach a target SE, then stop — collecting more data beyond that point provides little additional precision.

Data Science Case · Standard Error in A/B Testing at an E-Commerce Platform

An e-commerce company is testing a new checkout button (Treatment B) against the current design (Control A). The metric is conversion rate — the proportion of visitors who complete a purchase.

After running the test for one week, they observe:

  • Control A: $n_A = 5{,}000$ visitors, $s_A = 243$ conversions → $\hat{p}_A = 243/5000 = 0.0486$
  • Treatment B: $n_B = 5{,}000$ visitors, $s_B = 271$ conversions → $\hat{p}_B = 271/5000 = 0.0542$

The standard error for a proportion is $\text{SE}(\hat{p}) = \sqrt{p(1-p)/n}$, estimated as:

$$\widehat{\text{SE}}(\hat{p}_A) = \sqrt{\frac{0.0486 \times 0.9514}{5000}} \approx 0.00304, \qquad \widehat{\text{SE}}(\hat{p}_B) = \sqrt{\frac{0.0542 \times 0.9458}{5000}} \approx 0.00320$$

The observed lift is $0.0542 - 0.0486 = 0.0056$ (a 11.5% relative improvement). The SE of the difference is:

$$\text{SE}(\hat{p}_B - \hat{p}_A) = \sqrt{0.00304^2 + 0.00320^2} \approx 0.00441$$

The z-statistic is $z = 0.0056 / 0.00441 \approx 1.27$. Since $|z| < 1.96$, the result is not statistically significant at the 5% level — the observed improvement could plausibly be sampling noise. The team extends the test to $n = 15{,}000$ per group, which cuts the SE by $\sqrt{3}$ and gives the power needed to detect the effect reliably.

Figure 1.3. Left: Sampling distributions of $\bar{X}$ for six sample sizes, all centered at the true mean $\mu = 50$ (black vertical line) — confirming unbiasedness regardless of $n$. The distributions get progressively narrower with larger $n$. Right: The empirical standard error (colored dots, from simulation) falls almost exactly on the theoretical curve $\text{SE} = \sigma/\sqrt{n}$ (blue line). Note the characteristic $1/\sqrt{n}$ shape: steep initial drop, then diminishing returns.

In practice, $\sigma$ is unknown and must be estimated from the data. We use the sample standard deviation $s$ (defined in the next section) to get the estimated standard error:

$$\widehat{\text{SE}}(\bar{X}_n) = \frac{s}{\sqrt{n}}$$

This is what every statistical software package reports when you ask for the SE of a mean.

1.2.4 Estimating Population Variance — Why Divide by $n - 1$?

We now turn to estimating the population variance $\sigma^2$. The most natural estimator seems to be the "average squared deviation from the sample mean" — similar to how we would compute the variance of a dataset. But there is a subtle problem: using the sample mean $\bar{X}$ instead of the true mean $\mu$ introduces a systematic bias. The fix — dividing by $n - 1$ instead of $n$ — is one of the most frequently misunderstood results in introductory statistics.

Definition · Two Variance Estimators

Given sample $x_1, \ldots, x_n$, two common estimators for $\sigma^2$:

$$\hat{\sigma}^2_{\text{MLE}} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n(X_i - \bar{X})^2 \quad \text{(biased — divides by } n\text{)}$$ $$S^2 = \frac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^n(X_i - \bar{X})^2 \quad \text{(unbiased — divides by } n-1\text{)}$$

$S^2$ is called the sample variance and $S = \sqrt{S^2}$ is the sample standard deviation. These are the default in virtually all statistical software (e.g., numpy.std(x, ddof=1), R's var(x)).

Theorem 1.3 · $\mathbb{E}[S^2] = \sigma^2$  —  Bessel's Correction

The sample variance $S^2 = \frac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^n(X_i - \bar{X})^2$ is an unbiased estimator of $\sigma^2$: $\mathbb{E}[S^2] = \sigma^2$.

Equivalently: $\mathbb{E}[\hat{\sigma}^2_\text{MLE}] = \frac{n-1}{n}\sigma^2$, so $\hat{\sigma}^2_\text{MLE}$ has bias $-\sigma^2/n < 0$ — it systematically underestimates $\sigma^2$.

Proof — Step by Step

Before the algebra, understand why there is a bias. We are computing deviations $X_i - \bar{X}$, but we are using the sample mean $\bar{X}$, not the true mean $\mu$. The sample mean $\bar{X}$ is the value that minimizes $\sum(X_i - c)^2$ over all constants $c$. So $\sum(X_i - \bar{X})^2 \leq \sum(X_i - \mu)^2$ — always. The sum of squared deviations from $\bar{X}$ is systematically smaller than from $\mu$, so dividing by $n$ underestimates $\sigma^2$. Dividing by $n - 1$ corrects for this.

Step 1 — Algebraic identity. Expand $\sum(X_i - \bar{X})^2$:

$$\sum_{i=1}^n(X_i - \bar{X})^2 = \sum_{i=1}^n X_i^2 - 2\bar{X}\underbrace{\sum_{i=1}^n X_i}_{=\,n\bar{X}} + n\bar{X}^2 = \sum_{i=1}^n X_i^2 - n\bar{X}^2$$

Step 2 — Take expectations.

$$\mathbb{E}\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^n(X_i-\bar{X})^2\right] = \mathbb{E}\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^n X_i^2\right] - n\,\mathbb{E}[\bar{X}^2]$$

Step 3 — Compute $\mathbb{E}[X_i^2]$. Use $\text{Var}(Y) = \mathbb{E}[Y^2] - (\mathbb{E}[Y])^2$ rearranged to $\mathbb{E}[Y^2] = \text{Var}(Y) + (\mathbb{E}[Y])^2$:

$$\mathbb{E}[X_i^2] = \text{Var}(X_i) + (\mathbb{E}[X_i])^2 = \sigma^2 + \mu^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \mathbb{E}\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^n X_i^2\right] = n(\sigma^2 + \mu^2)$$

Step 4 — Compute $\mathbb{E}[\bar{X}^2]$. We know $\mathbb{E}[\bar{X}] = \mu$ and $\text{Var}(\bar{X}) = \sigma^2/n$, so:

$$\mathbb{E}[\bar{X}^2] = \text{Var}(\bar{X}) + (\mathbb{E}[\bar{X}])^2 = \frac{\sigma^2}{n} + \mu^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad n\,\mathbb{E}[\bar{X}^2] = \sigma^2 + n\mu^2$$

Step 5 — Subtract and conclude.

$$\mathbb{E}\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^n(X_i-\bar{X})^2\right] = n(\sigma^2 + \mu^2) - (\sigma^2 + n\mu^2) = n\sigma^2 - \sigma^2 = (n-1)\sigma^2$$ $$\mathbb{E}[S^2] = \mathbb{E}\!\left[\frac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^n(X_i-\bar{X})^2\right] = \frac{(n-1)\sigma^2}{n-1} = \sigma^2 \qquad \blacksquare$$
■
Intuition · Degrees of Freedom

The $n - 1$ has a beautiful interpretation: it counts the degrees of freedom in the deviations $X_1 - \bar{X}, X_2 - \bar{X}, \ldots, X_n - \bar{X}$.

Notice that these $n$ deviations must sum to zero: $\sum_{i=1}^n (X_i - \bar{X}) = 0$. This is always true — try it. This constraint means that once you know any $n - 1$ of the deviations, the last one is completely determined. There are only $n - 1$ truly free pieces of information about the spread in the data. We used one degree of freedom to estimate $\mu$ with $\bar{X}$, leaving only $n - 1$ for estimating $\sigma^2$.

Dividing by $n - 1$ is the correct accounting for how much independent information we actually have about variability.

Data Science Case · Variance Estimation in Model Evaluation

When evaluating a regression model on a test set of $n$ examples, you compute the residuals $\hat{\varepsilon}_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i$. The residual variance is:

$$s^2_\varepsilon = \frac{1}{n - k - 1}\sum_{i=1}^n \hat{\varepsilon}_i^2$$

where $k$ is the number of features. Here, $n - k - 1$ is the degrees of freedom: we estimated $k + 1$ parameters (the intercept plus $k$ coefficients), each costing one degree of freedom. In scikit-learn, this is what LinearRegression uses internally when computing standard errors of coefficients.

If you use $n$ instead of $n - k - 1$ (a common mistake in custom implementations), your estimated variance is biased downward. With $n = 50$ observations and $k = 20$ features, the bias is $50/(50-21) \approx 1.72$ — you underestimate the true error variance by 72%. This leads to false confidence in predictions.

Figure 1.4. Left: Bias of the two variance estimators as a function of sample size, computed from 8,000 simulation runs. The MLE estimator ($\div n$, red) is consistently below zero — it underestimates $\sigma^2$. The unbiased estimator ($\div (n-1)$, green) hugs the zero line. Right: A small sample of 8 points illustrating why the bias occurs — deviations from the sample mean $\bar{x}$ (blue) are always smaller than deviations from the true mean $\mu$ (gold), because $\bar{x}$ is the minimizer of sum-of-squares.

1.1.2 Degrees of Freedom and the Intuition

Intuition · Degrees of Freedom Explained

The $n-1$ in the denominator reflects the number of degrees of freedom — the number of independent pieces of information available to estimate $\sigma^2$.

The deviations $X_1 - \bar{X}, X_2 - \bar{X}, \ldots, X_n - \bar{X}$ satisfy the constraint $\sum_{i=1}^n(X_i - \bar{X}) = 0$. This is always true and trivially verified. This constraint means that once you know any $n-1$ deviations, the last one is completely determined. You have only $n-1$ free numbers, not $n$.

Why does the constraint exist? Because we used the same data to estimate $\mu$ via $\bar{X}$. That estimation "costs" one degree of freedom. We "spent" one df learning $\mu$, and only $n-1$ remain for learning $\sigma^2$. Dividing by $n-1$ accounts for this cost.

General rule: if you estimate $k$ parameters from the data before computing residuals, you have $n - k$ degrees of freedom for estimating variance.

Data Science Case · Degrees of Freedom in Regression

In linear regression with $k$ features (plus intercept), the residuals $\hat{\varepsilon}_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i$ have $n - (k+1)$ degrees of freedom. The unbiased estimator of the error variance is:

$$\hat{\sigma}^2_\varepsilon = \frac{1}{n-k-1}\sum_{i=1}^n \hat{\varepsilon}_i^2 \quad \text{(called "residual mean square" or "MSE")}$$

Using $n$ instead of $n-k-1$ underestimates $\sigma^2_\varepsilon$, making coefficient standard errors too small and t-statistics artificially large — leading to false positives (claiming significant features that aren't). With $n=50$ observations and $k=20$ features, the degrees of freedom adjustment $(n-k-1=29)$ vs. $(n=50)$ changes the variance estimate by a factor of $50/29 \approx 1.72$ — a massive difference that makes models appear more certain than they are.

Figure 1.4. Left: Bias of the two variance estimators as a function of $n$ (8,000 simulation runs each). The MLE (red, $\div n$) is systematically below zero; the unbiased estimator (green, $\div(n-1)$) centers on zero for all $n$. Middle: A small sample of 8 points illustrating the geometric reason for bias — deviations from $\bar{x}$ (green) are always smaller than deviations from the true $\mu$ (red), because $\bar{x}$ minimizes the sum of squares. The two sums of squares are displayed numerically. Right: Distribution of both estimators for $n=6$ over 5,000 simulations. Only $S^2$ is centered at the true $\sigma^2 = 16$ (gold dashed).

1.2.5 The Normal Approximation — The Central Limit Theorem

Knowing that $\bar{X}$ has mean $\mu$ and standard error $\sigma/\sqrt{n}$ characterizes the sampling distribution partially. To make probability statements — "there is a 95% chance that our estimate is within $k$ units of the truth" — we need to know the full shape of the distribution. The Central Limit Theorem provides this shape in the most elegant possible way.

Theorem 1.4 · Central Limit Theorem (Lindeberg-Lévy)

Let $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n$ be i.i.d. from any distribution with mean $\mu$ and finite variance $\sigma^2 > 0$. Define the standardized sample mean:

$$Z_n = \frac{\bar{X}_n - \mu}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}} = \frac{\sqrt{n}(\bar{X}_n - \mu)}{\sigma}$$

Then, regardless of the shape of the underlying distribution:

$$Z_n \xrightarrow{d} \mathcal{N}(0, 1) \quad \text{as } n \to \infty$$

That is, $Z_n$ converges in distribution to a standard normal. For large $n$, we can write the practical approximation:

$$\bar{X}_n \;\overset{\cdot}{\sim}\; \mathcal{N}\!\left(\mu,\; \frac{\sigma^2}{n}\right)$$
Intuition · Why the CLT Holds — And Why It Matters

Why it holds: Each observation $X_i$ makes a random contribution to the sum $\sum X_i$. As $n$ grows, these contributions — some positive, some negative, from all parts of the distribution — tend to cancel each other's extremes. The sum increasingly concentrates around $n\mu$, and its fluctuations, after scaling, follow a normal shape. The moment generating function proof makes this precise, but the intuition is: averaging out many small, independent, uncoordinated pushes produces a symmetric, bell-shaped distribution.

Why it is transformative: The CLT applies to data from any distribution — exponential, uniform, Poisson, heavy-tailed, whatever. You do not need to know or assume the population distribution to make valid probability statements about $\bar{X}$ for large $n$. This is why normal-distribution-based confidence intervals and hypothesis tests are so universally applicable in practice.

How large does $n$ need to be? Honest answer: it depends on the population. For roughly symmetric, light-tailed distributions (e.g., heights), $n = 10$ may suffice. For skewed distributions (e.g., incomes, transaction sizes), you may need $n \geq 50$ or more. For heavy-tailed distributions (e.g., Pareto-distributed wealth), the CLT can require very large $n$. The standard rule of thumb $n \geq 30$ is a compromise — check with simulation when in doubt.

Applying the CLT: The 95% Confidence Interval

The CLT directly enables confidence intervals. For large $n$, $\bar{X} \approx \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2/n)$, so the standardized version is approximately $\mathcal{N}(0,1)$. For the standard normal, $P(-1.96 \leq Z \leq 1.96) = 0.95$. Substituting and rearranging:

$$P\!\left(-1.96 \leq \frac{\bar{X}_n - \mu}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}} \leq 1.96\right) \approx 0.95$$
95% Confidence Interval for $\mu$
$$\left[\;\bar{X}_n - 1.96\cdot\frac{s}{\sqrt{n}},\quad \bar{X}_n + 1.96\cdot\frac{s}{\sqrt{n}}\;\right]$$

Interpretation: this procedure (collect data → compute $\bar{X}$ and $s$ → form the interval) will capture the true $\mu$ in approximately 95% of all applications. The interval itself — after the data is collected — is either right or wrong. The 95% refers to the long-run success rate of the procedure, not to any probability about a specific computed interval.

Data Science Case · CLT in Practice — Estimating Mean Model Latency

A machine learning engineer deploys a new inference model and wants to estimate its mean prediction latency $\mu$ (in milliseconds). She runs $n = 200$ benchmark requests and records the latencies.

import numpy as np
from scipy import stats

# Simulated latency data (log-normal in practice — right-skewed)
np.random.seed(42)
latencies = np.random.lognormal(mean=3.5, sigma=0.6, size=200)

n = len(latencies)
x_bar = latencies.mean()
s = latencies.std(ddof=1)          # unbiased sample std
se = s / np.sqrt(n)                # standard error

z = 1.96                           # 95% CI uses z = 1.96
ci_low  = x_bar - z * se
ci_high = x_bar + z * se

print(f"Sample mean:     {x_bar:.2f} ms")
print(f"Sample std:      {s:.2f} ms")
print(f"Standard error:  {se:.2f} ms")
print(f"95% CI:          ({ci_low:.2f}, {ci_high:.2f}) ms")

# Sample mean:     34.07 ms
# Sample std:      21.82 ms
# Standard error:   1.54 ms
# 95% CI:          (31.05, 37.09) ms

The individual latencies have std $\approx 21.8$ ms — very spread out. But the SE of $\bar{X}$ is only $1.54$ ms — our estimate of the mean is much more precise than individual observations, exactly as the formula predicts. The 95% CI is $[31.05, 37.09]$ ms, giving a tight bound on the true mean latency.

Notice: the raw data is log-normally distributed (skewed), but the CLT guarantees that $\bar{X}$ is approximately normal for $n = 200$, so the CI based on normality is valid.

1.3.3 How Large a Sample Is Needed?

Two related questions arise constantly in data science: (1) "Given $n$ observations, what precision can I achieve?" and (2) "To achieve a target precision, how large must $n$ be?" Both are answered by the standard error formula.

Margin of Error and Sample Size Formula

The margin of error $E$ for a $(1-\alpha)$ confidence interval is the half-width: $E = z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma/\sqrt{n}$. Solving for $n$:

$$n = \left(\frac{z_{\alpha/2}\cdot\sigma}{E}\right)^2$$

Since $\sigma$ is usually unknown, substitute a preliminary estimate $\hat{\sigma}$ (from pilot data or domain knowledge) and round up to the nearest integer.

Example 1.4 · Sample Size Calculation for a Data Science Study

A team wants to estimate the mean session duration on their platform to within $\pm 30$ seconds with 95% confidence. From previous data, $\hat{\sigma} \approx 4$ minutes $= 240$ seconds.

$$n \geq \left(\frac{1.960 \times 240}{30}\right)^2 = (15.68)^2 = 245.8 \quad \Rightarrow \quad n = 246$$

If they can only collect $n = 100$ sessions, the margin of error is: $E = 1.960 \times 240/\sqrt{100} = 47.0$ seconds — larger than desired.

If they want 99% confidence instead of 95%: $n \geq (2.576 \times 240/30)^2 = 425$. Higher confidence always requires more data for the same precision.

Intuition · The CLT Approximation in Practice

How large does $n$ need to be for the normal approximation to be reliable? The answer depends on how non-normal the population is:

  • Symmetric, light-tailed population: $n \geq 15$ is usually sufficient
  • Moderately skewed (e.g., exponential): $n \geq 30$ is a common minimum
  • Highly skewed (e.g., income, medical costs): $n \geq 50$ to $100$ may be needed
  • Heavy-tailed (e.g., Pareto with tail index close to 2): $n$ can be in the thousands
  • Discrete with extreme proportions (e.g., $p = 0.01$): Require $np \geq 5$ and $n(1-p) \geq 5$

The best practice: when in doubt, simulate the sampling distribution under your assumed model (as in Example 1.2) and visually check whether the normal approximation is adequate for your sample size.

Figure 1.3. Left: The standard error formula $\sigma/\sqrt{n}$ plotted against $n$. Colored dots are empirical SEs from simulation — they land almost exactly on the theoretical curve. The steep initial drop illustrates why the first 30–50 observations give the biggest improvement in precision. Right: Sampling distributions of $\bar{X}$ for four sample sizes from the same skewed population. The progression from $n=5$ (still skewed, wide) to $n=100$ (tightly concentrated, symmetric) shows the convergence to normality as the CLT predicts.

1.5 Sampling Distributions from a Normal Population

So far we have used the CLT to get approximate distributions for large $n$. When the population is exactly normal, we can derive the exact sampling distributions for any $n$ — no approximation needed. These exact results are essential for small-sample inference and form the basis of $t$-tests, $F$-tests, and chi-squared tests.

1.5.1 Distribution of the Sample Mean — Exact Result

Theorem 1.5 · Exact Distribution of $\bar{X}$ from a Normal Population

If $X_1, \ldots, X_n \overset{\text{iid}}{\sim} \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$, then:

$$\bar{X}_n \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(\mu,\; \frac{\sigma^2}{n}\right) \quad \text{(exactly, for all } n \geq 1\text{)}$$
Proof

A linear combination of independent normal random variables is exactly normal. $\bar{X}_n = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n X_i$ is a linear combination of the independent normals $X_1, \ldots, X_n$. By the linearity property of normal distributions:

$$\bar{X}_n \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n \mu,\; \frac{1}{n^2}\sum_{i=1}^n\sigma^2\right) = \mathcal{N}\!\left(\mu,\; \frac{\sigma^2}{n}\right) \qquad \blacksquare$$

This is stronger than the CLT: the CLT says $\bar{X}_n$ is approximately normal for large $n$ regardless of the population. Theorem 1.5 says $\bar{X}_n$ is exactly normal for all $n$ when the population is normal. ■

1.5.2 The Chi-Squared Distribution of $S^2$

Theorem 1.6 · Distribution of the Sample Variance from a Normal Population

If $X_1, \ldots, X_n \overset{\text{iid}}{\sim} \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$, then:

$$\frac{(n-1)S^2}{\sigma^2} \sim \chi^2(n-1)$$

where $\chi^2(k)$ denotes the chi-squared distribution with $k$ degrees of freedom.

Proof Sketch

Define standardized variables $Z_i = (X_i - \mu)/\sigma \overset{\text{iid}}{\sim} \mathcal{N}(0,1)$. By definition, $\sum_{i=1}^n Z_i^2 \sim \chi^2(n)$.

Decompose $\sum Z_i^2$ by adding and subtracting $\bar{Z} = \bar{X}/\sigma - \mu/\sigma$:

$$\sum_{i=1}^n Z_i^2 = \sum_{i=1}^n(Z_i - \bar{Z})^2 + n\bar{Z}^2$$

The term $n\bar{Z}^2 = n(\bar{X}-\mu)^2/\sigma^2 \sim \chi^2(1)$ (square of a standard normal). The first term equals $(n-1)S^2/\sigma^2$. Since we subtract one degree of freedom (for estimating $\mu$ with $\bar{X}$), $(n-1)S^2/\sigma^2 \sim \chi^2(n-1)$. The independence of these two terms (established below) confirms the degree counting. $\blacksquare$ ■

Intuition · Chi-Squared as Sum of Squared Normals

If $Z_1, \ldots, Z_k$ are i.i.d. $\mathcal{N}(0,1)$, then $\sum_{i=1}^k Z_i^2 \sim \chi^2(k)$. This is the defining property of the chi-squared distribution.

The result $(n-1)S^2/\sigma^2 \sim \chi^2(n-1)$ says: the standardized sample variance behaves like a sum of $n-1$ squared standard normals. We "lose" one degree of freedom because $S^2$ uses deviations from $\bar{X}$ (estimated from data) rather than from $\mu$ (known). The deviation from $\bar{X}$ lives in an $(n-1)$-dimensional space — hence $n-1$ degrees of freedom.

1.5.3 Joint Distribution of $\bar{X}$ and $S^2$ — Independence

Theorem 4.7 · Independence of $\bar{X}$ and $S^2$ (Normal Population)

If $X_1, \ldots, X_n \overset{\text{iid}}{\sim} \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$, then $\bar{X}$ and $S^2$ are statistically independent.

Proof Sketch — Via Orthogonal Decomposition

Write the data vector $\mathbf{X} = (X_1, \ldots, X_n)^\top$ and the all-ones vector $\mathbf{1} = (1, \ldots, 1)^\top/\sqrt{n}$. The sample mean is $\bar{X} = \mathbf{1}^\top\mathbf{X}$ — the projection of $\mathbf{X}$ onto the subspace spanned by $\mathbf{1}$. The deviations $X_i - \bar{X}$ form the residual vector, which is the projection of $\mathbf{X}$ onto the orthogonal complement of $\mathbf{1}$.

Since $\mathbf{X}$ is multivariate normal, its projections onto orthogonal subspaces are independent. Therefore $\bar{X}$ (projection onto $\mathbf{1}$) is independent of $(X_1 - \bar{X}, \ldots, X_n - \bar{X})$ (projection onto the orthogonal complement), and hence independent of $S^2$. $\blacksquare$ ■

This independence is unique to the normal distribution. For non-normal populations, $\bar{X}$ and $S^2$ are generally not independent. It is a special property that enables exact inference from normal populations and underlies the $t$-test and $F$-test.

1.5.4 Student's t Distribution

In practice, $\sigma^2$ is unknown and must be replaced by $S^2$. What happens to the distribution of the standardized mean?

Theorem 4.8 · Student's t Distribution

If $X_1, \ldots, X_n \overset{\text{iid}}{\sim} \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$ with both $\mu$ and $\sigma^2$ unknown, then:

$$T = \frac{\bar{X}_n - \mu}{S/\sqrt{n}} \sim t(n-1)$$

where $t(k)$ denotes Student's $t$ distribution with $k$ degrees of freedom.

Proof

Write $T$ as a ratio using the results established above:

$$T = \frac{\bar{X} - \mu}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}} \cdot \frac{1}{S/\sigma} = \frac{Z}{\sqrt{V/(n-1)}}$$

where $Z = (\bar{X}-\mu)/(\sigma/\sqrt{n}) \sim \mathcal{N}(0,1)$ (Theorem 1.5) and $V = (n-1)S^2/\sigma^2 \sim \chi^2(n-1)$ (Theorem 1.6), and $Z \perp V$ (Theorem 4.7).

The ratio $Z/\sqrt{V/k}$ where $Z \sim \mathcal{N}(0,1)$ and $V \sim \chi^2(k)$ are independent is, by definition, a $t$ distribution with $k$ degrees of freedom. Here $k = n-1$. $\blacksquare$ ■

Intuition · t vs. Normal — Why the Heavier Tails?

When $\sigma$ is known, we standardize by $\sigma/\sqrt{n}$ and get exactly $\mathcal{N}(0,1)$. When $\sigma$ is unknown and estimated by $S$, we introduce additional randomness: $S$ might happen to be smaller than $\sigma$, making the denominator too small and the whole ratio larger than it should be. This extra randomness in the denominator inflates the tails.

As $n \to \infty$, $S \to \sigma$ (by the Law of Large Numbers), and the extra randomness vanishes — the $t(n-1)$ distribution converges to $\mathcal{N}(0,1)$. For practical purposes, $t(30) \approx \mathcal{N}(0,1)$.

The t distribution is not just an approximation for when $n$ is small — it is the exact distribution of $T$ for all $n \geq 2$ when sampling from a normal population. This exactness is what makes $t$-tests valid even with $n = 5$ or $n = 10$.

Example 1.5 · Using the t Distribution for Small-Sample Inference

A quality engineer measures the breaking strength of $n = 12$ cable samples from a production run. The sample mean is $\bar{x} = 847.3$ N and $s = 31.6$ N. Construct a 95% confidence interval for the true mean breaking strength.

Since $n = 12$ is small and the population can reasonably be assumed normal (manufacturing processes typically produce normal measurements), we use the $t$ distribution with $n-1 = 11$ degrees of freedom.

From a $t$ table or Python: $t_{0.025, 11} = 2.201$

$$\bar{x} \pm t_{0.025,11} \cdot \frac{s}{\sqrt{n}} = 847.3 \pm 2.201 \times \frac{31.6}{\sqrt{12}} = 847.3 \pm 20.1 = [827.2,\; 867.4] \text{ N}$$

If we had used $z_{0.025} = 1.960$ instead of $t_{0.025,11} = 2.201$, the CI would be $[829.4, 865.2]$ — too narrow by about 10% per side. With only 12 observations, using the $z$ distribution underestimates uncertainty.

Figure 1.5. Sampling distributions from a normal population. Left: The chi-squared distribution of $(n-1)S^2/\sigma^2$ for four degrees of freedom — note the right skew that decreases as $df$ grows. Center: The $t$ distribution vs. the standard normal — the heavier tails are clearly visible for small $df$, converging to normal as $df$ increases. Right: Simulation confirmation that $({\bar{X}-\mu})/(S/\sqrt{n})$ follows $t(n-1)$ exactly for $n=8$ — the simulated histogram matches the theoretical $t(7)$ density (red) far better than the normal (blue dashed).

1.6 Sampling from a Finite Population

All of our results so far assumed sampling from an infinite population (or equivalently, sampling with replacement from a finite one). When we sample without replacement from a finite population of size $N$, the formula for the variance of $\bar{X}$ needs a correction.

1.6.1 The Finite Population Correction Factor

Theorem 4.9 · Variance of $\bar{X}$ Under Simple Random Sampling Without Replacement

If a sample of size $n$ is drawn without replacement from a finite population of size $N$ with variance $\sigma^2$, then:

$$\text{Var}(\bar{X}_n) = \frac{\sigma^2}{n}\cdot\underbrace{\frac{N-n}{N-1}}_{\text{FPC}}$$

The term $\text{FPC} = \sqrt{\frac{N-n}{N-1}}$ is the Finite Population Correction factor. For large $N$: $\text{FPC} \approx \sqrt{1 - f}$ where $f = n/N$ is the sampling fraction.

Proof Sketch

For sampling without replacement, the $X_i$ are no longer independent — knowing $X_1$ changes the distribution of $X_2$ slightly, because $X_1$ can no longer appear in the remaining draws. The covariance between any two observations is:

$$\text{Cov}(X_i, X_j) = -\frac{\sigma^2}{N-1} \quad \text{for } i \neq j$$

The negative covariance reflects the fact that sampling without replacement introduces slight negative correlation — a high $X_i$ means the remaining pool has slightly lower values. Then:

$$\text{Var}(\bar{X}) = \frac{1}{n^2}\!\left[n\sigma^2 + n(n-1)\cdot\left(-\frac{\sigma^2}{N-1}\right)\right] = \frac{\sigma^2}{n}\cdot\frac{N-n}{N-1} \qquad \blacksquare \qquad \square$$

1.6.2 When Does the FPC Matter?

Intuition · When the FPC Is Important

The FPC is essentially 1 (i.e., negligible) when the sampling fraction $f = n/N$ is small. Specifically:

  • $f < 5\%$: FPC $> 0.975$ — the infinite-population formula is accurate to within 2.5%
  • $f = 10\%$: FPC $\approx 0.949$ — variance is reduced by about 5% vs. infinite-population formula
  • $f = 50\%$: FPC $\approx 0.707$ — variance is reduced by 50% — quite significant
  • $f = 100\%$ (census): FPC $= 0$ — zero variance, because you know the whole population!

Rule of thumb: Apply the FPC when the sampling fraction $f = n/N$ exceeds 5%. For most large-scale data science applications (millions of users, billions of transactions), $f$ is negligible and the FPC can be ignored. For small, well-defined populations (all 500 employees in a company, all 2,000 students in a school), the FPC provides a meaningful variance reduction.

Data Science Case · FPC in Organizational Surveys

A company of $N = 800$ employees conducts an engagement survey. They send it to $n = 200$ randomly selected employees — a sampling fraction of $f = 200/800 = 25\%$.

Suppose satisfaction scores have $\sigma = 1.5$ on a 1–5 scale.

Without FPC: $\text{SE} = 1.5/\sqrt{200} = 0.106$

With FPC: $\text{SE} = (1.5/\sqrt{200})\cdot\sqrt{(800-200)/(800-1)} = 0.106 \times 0.866 = 0.0918$

The 95% CI without FPC: $\bar{x} \pm 0.208$. With FPC: $\bar{x} \pm 0.180$. The FPC makes the interval noticeably narrower, reflecting the real reduction in uncertainty from sampling a quarter of the population.

Figure 1.6. Left: Standard error for sampling without replacement from a finite population ($N=500$, $\sigma=10$). The red curve (no FPC) overestimates SE when $n$ is a substantial fraction of $N$. The blue curve (with FPC) matches the simulation (gold dots) almost perfectly. The gray line marks $n = N/2$. Right: The FPC factor $\sqrt{1-f}$ as a function of sampling fraction $f = n/N$. Below 5% sampling fraction (left of leftmost dot), FPC $> 0.975$ and is negligible. At high sampling fractions, ignoring FPC gives misleadingly wide intervals.

4.7 Sampling Methods

1.2.6 Sampling Methods: Simple Random, Stratified, Cluster, Systematic

So far we have assumed data arrives via simple random sampling. In practice, data scientists and statisticians choose a sampling method deliberately — different methods have different costs, precision, and applicability. The choice of sampling method is a critical design decision that affects every downstream analysis.

Method 1 — Simple Random Sampling (SRS)

In simple random sampling, each unit in the population has an equal probability of being selected, and all units are selected independently. The sample of size $n$ is drawn uniformly at random from all $\binom{N}{n}$ possible subsets of the population.

Simple Random Sampling — Properties
  • Unbiased: $\mathbb{E}[\bar{X}] = \mu$ — correct on average for all population subgroups
  • SE formula: $\text{SE}(\bar{X}) = \sigma/\sqrt{n}$ (approximately, for large populations)
  • Requires: A complete, accurate list of the population (a sampling frame)
  • Drawback: May under-represent small but important subgroups by chance
Data Science Case · SRS for Model Evaluation

When splitting a dataset into train/validation/test sets for model evaluation, the standard approach is SRS: randomly select (say) 70% of rows for training, 15% for validation, 15% for test. This ensures that the test set is an unbiased sample from the original data distribution, so test performance estimates the true deployment performance.

Pitfall: SRS can create imbalanced class splits in classification problems. If your data has 2% positive examples, a random 15% test set might have 1% or 3% positives by chance, making test performance estimates noisy. Stratified sampling solves this.

Method 2 — Stratified Sampling

In stratified sampling, the population is divided into non-overlapping subgroups (strata), and a simple random sample is drawn independently from each stratum. The strata are defined by a variable known before sampling — e.g., age group, product category, or geographic region.

Stratified Sampling — Properties

Suppose the population has $K$ strata with sizes $N_1, \ldots, N_K$ and within-stratum variances $\sigma^2_1, \ldots, \sigma^2_K$. Draw $n_k$ units from stratum $k$. The stratified estimator is:

$$\bar{X}_{\text{strat}} = \sum_{k=1}^K \frac{N_k}{N}\bar{X}_k$$

Its variance is:

$$\text{Var}(\bar{X}_{\text{strat}}) = \sum_{k=1}^K \left(\frac{N_k}{N}\right)^2 \frac{\sigma^2_k}{n_k}$$

Key advantage: If strata are more homogeneous than the overall population (within-stratum variance $\sigma^2_k <$ population variance $\sigma^2$), stratified sampling achieves lower SE than SRS for the same total $n$.

Data Science Case · Stratified Train/Test Split

In scikit-learn, train_test_split(X, y, stratify=y) uses stratified sampling to ensure that the proportion of each class in the training and test sets matches the overall proportion. This guarantees balanced evaluation even for imbalanced datasets.

from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split

# Without stratification (SRS) — class proportions may differ
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X, y, test_size=0.2)
print(y_test.mean())   # might be 0.018 when true proportion is 0.02

# With stratification — class proportions are preserved
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X, y, test_size=0.2, stratify=y)
print(y_test.mean())   # guaranteed to be very close to 0.02

In survey design, stratified sampling ensures adequate representation of small but important groups. For example, estimating customer satisfaction across 50 countries, you would stratify by country so that even small-population countries (e.g., Luxembourg) get enough representation for reliable country-level estimates.

Method 3 — Cluster Sampling

In cluster sampling, the population is divided into clusters (groups of units), a random selection of clusters is chosen, and either all units within the selected clusters are measured (one-stage) or a random sample within each selected cluster is taken (two-stage).

Cluster Sampling — Properties and Trade-offs
  • Efficiency: Dramatically reduces data collection cost when units within clusters are geographically or logistically proximate
  • Statistical cost: Units within a cluster are usually similar (correlated), so cluster sampling is less statistically efficient than SRS for the same total $n$
  • Intraclass correlation (ICC): Quantifies how correlated units within a cluster are. High ICC → cluster sampling less efficient
  • Design effect (DEFF): $\text{DEFF} = \text{Var}_{\text{cluster}} / \text{Var}_{\text{SRS}} \geq 1$. Need $\text{DEFF} \times n_\text{SRS}$ units with cluster sampling to match SRS precision
Data Science Case · Cluster Sampling in Distributed ML Training

When training on data stored across many servers, it is often infeasible to draw a uniform random sample. Instead, you sample servers (clusters) at random and use all data on the selected servers. This is two-stage cluster sampling: first sample servers, then sample records within servers.

The statistical consequence: if records on the same server are correlated (e.g., they come from the same geographic region), the effective sample size is smaller than the number of records. The design effect can be 1.5–3 in practice, meaning you need 1.5–3× more total records to achieve the same model quality as a true random sample of the same size. This is a real cost that distributed ML systems must account for in their data collection budgets.

Method 4 — Systematic Sampling

In systematic sampling, units are selected at regular intervals from the population. With a sampling interval $k = N/n$, you choose a random starting point between 1 and $k$, then select every $k$-th unit thereafter.

Systematic Sampling — Properties and Risks
  • Practical advantage: Simple to implement — no need for a complete list or random number generator; just take every $k$-th item off a production line, every $k$-th row in a log file, etc.
  • Usually similar to SRS: For most populations, systematic sampling has approximately the same statistical properties as SRS
  • Critical risk — periodicity: If the population has a periodic pattern with the same period as $k$, systematic sampling can be catastrophically biased or can have zero variance (which sounds good but means you are always hitting the same type of unit)
Warning · The Periodicity Trap in Systematic Sampling

Suppose you are analyzing web server logs and sampling every $k = 24$-th record to estimate hourly traffic patterns. If logs contain one entry per hour and your sampling interval is exactly 24, you will always sample at the same hour of the day — completely missing the within-day variation. Your estimate of "average hourly traffic" will actually be the traffic at one specific hour, not the daily average.

In data science: Systematic sampling of rows from a database is dangerous if the data was inserted in a cyclical pattern (e.g., daily batch jobs). Always plot the sampled data over time before trusting systematic sampling results.

Figure 1.5. Visual comparison of four sampling methods on a population of 100 units (dots). Gold dots are selected units. Simple random: uniform, unclustered selection with no structure. Stratified: population divided into four subgroups (colors), proportional samples drawn from each — ensures coverage of all groups. Cluster: population divided into spatial clusters; a random subset of clusters selected (gold boxes), all units within selected clusters included. Systematic: units on a regular grid, every 4th unit selected — regular but potentially vulnerable to periodicity.
MethodHow units are selectedBest whenWatch out for
Simple Random Each unit equally likely, all independent Complete population list available; population is homogeneous May under-represent rare groups; requires a sampling frame
Stratified Random sample within each predefined stratum Population has known, important subgroups; rare groups need representation Requires knowing strata before sampling; complex analysis
Cluster Random sample of groups; measure units within groups Population naturally in clusters; geographic or logistic constraints Less efficient if units within clusters are similar (high ICC)
Systematic Every $k$-th unit from an ordered list No complete list available; convenient and fast Catastrophic if population has periodicity matching interval $k$

1.2.7 Sampling Bias — And Why It Destroys Inference

We have discussed how to collect valid random samples. Now we must confront what happens when we fail — when the sample is not a representative draw from the target population. This is sampling bias, and it is arguably the most dangerous failure mode in data science, because unlike random error, it does not average out with more data.

Definition · Sampling Bias

Sampling bias occurs when the mechanism that determines which units appear in the sample is systematically related to the quantity being measured, causing the sample distribution to differ from the population distribution. Formally, the sample is drawn from a distribution $F_\phi \neq F_\theta$ — a different distribution than the population of interest.

The devastating consequence: $\mathbb{E}[\hat{\theta}] = \phi \neq \theta$. The estimator is biased no matter how large the sample is. More data amplifies the same wrong signal — it does not fix the problem.

The Four Most Common Sources of Sampling Bias

1. Selection Bias. The probability of being included in the sample depends on the value of the variable being measured. Example: surveying hospital patients to estimate the rate of a disease in the general population overestimates prevalence because sick people are more likely to be in hospitals. In data science: scraped social media data over-represents active users, who may behave very differently from passive ones.

2. Survivorship Bias. You observe only units that "survived" some selection process, and you treat them as representative of all units. Example: studying the characteristics of successful startups to understand what makes startups succeed ignores all the failed startups with the same characteristics. In data science: training a churn-prediction model only on current customers ignores the already-churned customers who might have had the most distinctive patterns.

3. Non-Response Bias. Certain types of units systematically fail to respond or participate, making the observed sample unrepresentative. Example: online surveys about website satisfaction are completed mostly by people who feel strongly (either very happy or very unhappy), not the silent majority. In data science: user feedback for a product feature is dominated by power users, not the typical user.

4. Convenience Sampling. Units are selected because they are easy to access, not because they are representative. Example: asking colleagues for opinions represents a very specific social and professional network. In data science: using a historical dataset collected for a different purpose as proxy for the target population (temporal distribution shift, demographic mismatch, etc.).

Warning · More Data Cannot Fix Sampling Bias

This is the single most important insight about sampling bias, and it cannot be overstated. If your sample is biased — if you are systematically over-representing certain types of units — then collecting more data from the same biased process just gives you a more precise wrong answer.

Formally: if your estimator has bias $b = \mathbb{E}[\hat{\theta}] - \theta$, the MSE is:

$$\text{MSE}(\hat{\theta}) = b^2 + \frac{\sigma^2}{n}$$

As $n \to \infty$, the variance term $\sigma^2/n \to 0$ — but the $b^2$ term remains constant. You converge to the wrong answer with increasing precision.

Figure 1.6. Illustration of sampling bias using income estimation. Left: A random sample (green) produces a distribution centered near the true mean $\mu$ (gold dashed), while a biased sample that over-represents high earners (red) produces a distribution shifted dramatically to the right. Both samples have $n = 500$, yet the biased sample estimates income as nearly twice the truth. Right: as sample size grows, the random sample mean (green) converges to the true $\mu$, but the biased sample mean (red) converges to a systematically wrong value — more data makes it more precisely wrong.
Data Science Case · Survivorship Bias in Financial Modeling

A quantitative analyst builds a model to predict stock returns using data from the S&P 500. She trains on all companies currently in the index (as of today) and achieves impressive backtest performance. What went wrong?

The S&P 500 as it exists today contains only companies that survived and prospered over the past decades. Companies that went bankrupt, were acquired, or performed so poorly they were delisted are not in today's index. By training on survivors only, the analyst's model has learned patterns that characterize survival — and since most historical periods are in the sample, it looks like those patterns predicted future performance. In reality, this is survivorship bias: she never saw the patterns associated with failure.

The fix: use a point-in-time index constituent list, which records which companies were in the index on each historical date, including companies that later failed. This is standard practice in quantitative finance and a requirement for any credible backtest.

Data Science Case · Training Data Bias in Machine Learning

A team builds a facial recognition model trained on a large, "diverse" dataset scraped from the internet. The model achieves 97% accuracy on the test set (also scraped from the internet). When deployed on passport control cameras at a border crossing, accuracy drops to 83% for certain demographic groups.

The problem: images scraped from the internet over-represent younger people (who use social media more), people from wealthier countries (who have better internet access), and people in well-lit environments (professional photos). The operational population — all travelers at a border crossing — has a very different demographic composition and image quality distribution. The training sample was not drawn from the same distribution as the deployment population.

This is not just a machine learning problem — it is a sampling bias problem. The data collection process systematically excluded (or under-represented) exactly the subgroups where the model failed. No amount of architectural tuning or hyperparameter optimization can fix a fundamentally biased training set.

Diagnosis tools:

  • Compare feature distributions between training data and deployment data (covariate shift detection)
  • Report performance broken down by subgroups, not just overall accuracy
  • Use calibration plots to check whether predicted probabilities match observed frequencies across demographic groups

✦ Chapter Summary

This chapter built the foundations of statistical inference from first principles. The central thread: we want to learn about a population parameter $\theta$ but can only observe a random sample. The sampling distribution — the distribution of our estimator across all possible samples — is the key tool for understanding what our sample can and cannot tell us.

Parameter vs. Estimator

$\theta$ is fixed and unknown. $\hat{\theta}_n = T(X_1,\ldots,X_n)$ is a random variable. The estimate $\hat{\theta}$ (specific number from data) vs. the estimator (the rule) is a crucial distinction. Greek letters = unknown; Roman letters/hats = computed.

i.i.d. Assumption

Identically distributed = representative sampling. Independent = no correlation between observations. Both are needed for the variance formula $\text{Var}(\bar{X}) = \sigma^2/n$. Violations — correlated data, biased sampling frames — break all classical inference.

Unbiasedness of $\bar{X}$

$\mathbb{E}[\bar{X}_n] = \mu$ — proved via linearity of expectation. Requires only identical distributions, not independence. Means the sampling distribution is centered at the truth, not that each estimate is correct.

Standard Error

$\text{SE}(\bar{X}_n) = \sigma/\sqrt{n}$ — proved using independence of observations. Halving SE requires 4× the data. In practice: $\widehat{\text{SE}} = s/\sqrt{n}$. SE is not the same as $\sigma$ — it measures spread of estimates, not of data.

Bessel's Correction — Why $n-1$

$\hat{\sigma}^2_\text{MLE} = \frac{1}{n}\sum(X_i-\bar{X})^2$ underestimates $\sigma^2$ by factor $(n-1)/n$ because $\bar{X}$ minimizes sum of squared deviations. Dividing by $n-1$ corrects this. This uses up one degree of freedom for estimating $\mu$.

Central Limit Theorem

$\bar{X}_n \overset{\cdot}{\sim} \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2/n)$ for large $n$, regardless of population shape. Enables 95% CI: $\bar{X} \pm 1.96\cdot s/\sqrt{n}$. Requires finite variance. "Large enough" depends on skewness; $n \geq 30$ is a rough guide.

Exact Distributions (Normal Population)

$\bar{X} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu,\sigma^2/n)$ exactly. $(n-1)S^2/\sigma^2 \sim \chi^2(n-1)$. $\bar{X} \perp S^2$ (unique to normal populations). The pivot $T = (\bar{X}-\mu)/(S/\sqrt{n}) \sim t(n-1)$ — heavier tails than normal for small $n$, converging to normal as $n \to \infty$.

Finite Population Correction

Without replacement from $N$ units: $\text{Var}(\bar{X}) = \frac{\sigma^2}{n}\cdot\frac{N-n}{N-1}$. Negligible when $f = n/N < 5\%$. Significant when sampling a substantial fraction of a small population. As $n \to N$: variance $\to 0$ (census).

Sampling Methods

SRS: unbiased, needs complete frame. Stratified: efficient when strata differ, guaranteed group representation. Cluster: cost-efficient but statistically inefficient (high ICC = lower effective $n$). Systematic: convenient but dangerous if population is periodic.

Sampling Bias

Occurs when sample is drawn from $F_\phi \neq F_\theta$. Sources: selection bias, survivorship bias, non-response bias, convenience sampling. $\text{MSE} = b^2 + \sigma^2/n$ — the bias term $b^2$ does not vanish with $n$. More data amplifies a biased signal rather than correcting it.

Exercises

Exercise 1.1 · Sampling Distributions by Simulation
Exploring the CLT Empirically

Write Python code to simulate the sampling distribution of $\bar{X}$ from a $\text{Uniform}(0, 10)$ population for $n = 2, 5, 30, 100$. For each $n$: (a) Plot the histogram of 10,000 sample means alongside the theoretical normal approximation $\mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2/n)$. (b) Compute the mean and standard deviation of the sample means and compare to the theoretical values $\mu = 5$ and $\sigma/\sqrt{n} = (10/\sqrt{12})/\sqrt{n}$. (c) At what $n$ does the normal approximation become visually indistinguishable from the histogram? How does your answer change if you use an exponential population instead?

Exercise 1.2 · Standard Error and Confidence Intervals
API Latency Estimation

A data engineer records $n = 150$ response times (in ms) from a production API endpoint. The sample mean is $\bar{x} = 42.3$ ms and the sample standard deviation is $s = 18.7$ ms. (a) Compute the standard error $\widehat{\text{SE}}(\bar{X})$. (b) Construct a 95% confidence interval for the true mean latency $\mu$. (c) The team's SLA requires mean latency below 45 ms. Does the CI support that they are meeting this SLA? Explain why a single measured value cannot answer this question but the CI can. (d) How many observations would they need to reduce the margin of error to $\pm 1$ ms at the 95% level?

Exercise 1.3 · Bessel's Correction
Understanding Degrees of Freedom

(a) Prove algebraically that $\sum_{i=1}^n(X_i - \bar{X}) = 0$ for any sample. Why does this imply that the $n$ deviations have only $n-1$ degrees of freedom? (b) For a sample of 6 values $\{5, 9, 3, 12, 7, 8\}$, compute both $\hat{\sigma}^2_\text{MLE}$ and $S^2$. What is the percentage difference? (c) From the proof of Theorem 1.3, the bias of $\hat{\sigma}^2_\text{MLE}$ is $-\sigma^2/n$. For $n = 5$ and $\sigma^2 = 25$, what is this bias? At what $n$ does the bias fall below 1% of $\sigma^2$?

Exercise 1.4 · Sampling Methods in Practice
Designing a User Survey

A product team wants to estimate average weekly app usage (hours) across their 500,000 users. They know users fall into three groups: Heavy users (10% of users, avg 15 hrs/week), Regular users (60%, avg 3 hrs/week), Light users (30%, avg 0.5 hrs/week). (a) What is the true population mean $\mu$? (b) If they draw a SRS of $n = 200$ users, what is the SE of their estimate? (c) If they use stratified sampling with proportional allocation (sampling 10%, 60%, 30% of their 200 from each group), what is the SE? (d) Compare the two SEs and explain the difference intuitively.

Exercise 1.5 · Sampling Bias Diagnosis
Bias in ML Training Data

A team trains a credit default prediction model using loan application data from the past five years. They achieve 94% accuracy on a held-out test set from the same period. When deployed, the model has 78% accuracy. (a) List at least three specific sources of sampling bias that might explain this gap. For each, describe what type of bias it is (selection, survivorship, non-response, or convenience) and how it would affect the training distribution vs. the deployment distribution. (b) What data would you collect, and what statistical tests would you run, to diagnose whether temporal distribution shift is responsible? (c) Why would simply collecting more historical loan data not necessarily fix the problem?
